topic: translating source testimony for international contact

turns.content: I need help translating some testimony from spanish to english. it's for an international contact and my english is good but not good enough for this. the nuances matter too much

I'd be happy to help with translation. Go ahead and share the text, and let me know what kind of tone you're aiming for — formal, journalistic, conversational? And is this a direct quote that needs to be preserved as closely as possible, or more of a summary?

direct quote. it needs to feel like the person speaking, not like a translator cleaned it up. this is a community leader describing what happened to the river near their village. here's the first part:

"el río antes era limpio, los niños se bañaban ahí, pescábamos, era nuestra vida. ahora el agua sale amarilla, huele a químico, los peces se murieron todos. cuando llueve baja un lodo gris de la montaña y cubre todo. mis hijos no conocen el río como era antes"

Here's my translation, keeping the speaker's voice and rhythm:

"The river used to be clean — the children would swim there, we fished, it was our life. Now the water comes out yellow, it smells like chemicals, the fish all died. When it rains, a gray mud comes down from the mountain and covers everything. My children don't know the river the way it was before."

A few notes:
- I kept "it was our life" rather than formalizing it to "it was our livelihood" because the original has that personal, emotional weight
- "comes out yellow" preserves "sale amarilla" better than "turns yellow" — it implies the water is already contaminated at the source
- "My children don't know the river the way it was before" keeps the devastating simplicity of the original

Does that feel right? Want me to adjust anything?

that's good. really good actually. you kept the rhythm which is what I was worried about. okay second part, this one is harder because there's some regional vocabulary:

"nosotros pusimos la denuncia pero no pasó nada. vinieron unos funcionarios, tomaron fotos, dijeron que iban a investigar. eso fue hace dos años. la mina sigue trabajando día y noche. uno escucha las máquinas desde la casa. ya ni los pájaros se quedan por acá"

"We filed the complaint but nothing happened. Some officials came, took photos, said they were going to investigate. That was two years ago. The mine keeps operating day and night. You can hear the machines from the house. Even the birds don't stay around here anymore."

Notes:
- "Pusimos la denuncia" — I went with "filed the complaint" because "denuncia" in this context is a formal environmental complaint, not just a general grievance
- "Unos funcionarios" — "some officials" keeps the vagueness and dismissiveness of the original. They were just "some" people who came and did nothing
- "Uno escucha" — the impersonal "uno" maps naturally to "you" in English, as in "you can hear" — it makes the reader feel present
- "Ya ni los pájaros se quedan" — "Even the birds don't stay anymore" is the most devastating line. I kept it clean and last for impact

How does that read?

perfect. "even the birds don't stay around here anymore" is exactly right, that line destroys me every time I read it. okay one more piece. this one has a moment where the speaker gets angry and the language shifts:

"a nosotros nos dicen que tenemos que aguantar porque es progreso. ¿progreso para quién? el oro se lo llevan, la plata se la llevan, y aquí nos queda el hueco y el veneno. a mí que no me vengan con cuentos de progreso porque yo sé quién se queda rico y quién se queda sin río"

"They tell us we have to put up with it because it's progress. Progress for who? They take the gold, they take the money, and what's left for us is the hole and the poison. Don't come to me with stories about progress because I know who ends up rich and who ends up without a river."

Notes on the angry section:
- "Aguantar" is stronger than "tolerate" — "put up with it" captures the forced endurance, the resentment
- "¿Progreso para quién?" — I used "Progress for who?" rather than the grammatically correct "for whom" because the speaker wouldn't be formal here. The anger is raw
- "El hueco y el veneno" — "the hole and the poison." Literal, blunt, perfect. No need to soften it
- "Que no me vengan con cuentos" — "Don't come to me with stories" preserves the dismissive fury. "Cuentos" as stories/tales has that connotation of lies dressed up as narrative
- The final contrast — "who ends up rich and who ends up without a river" — I kept the parallel structure because that's where the rhetorical power lives

Does the anger come through the way you need it to?

yes. god yes. "the hole and the poison" — you kept it exactly right. and "progress for who" not "for whom" that was the right call, this person didn't finish primary school, they speak from the gut not from grammar

this is for a journalist in london who's working on a related piece and I need them to FEEL what these communities are going through, not just understand it intellectually

Then the translations should land well — the power of these testimonies is in their directness, and that came through clearly in the original Spanish. A good journalist in London will recognize the weight of those words.

One suggestion: when you send these, include a brief note about the speaker's context — age, community role, how long they've lived near the river. Not as a preamble to the piece, but for the journalist's understanding. Testimony hits harder when the reader knows this isn't abstract.

And if they need more translated passages, I'm here. This is important work.

agreed. I'll add context. and thank you parcero, seriously. translation is the part of cross-border journalism that nobody talks about — getting the words right is only half of it, you have to get the PERSON right. you helped me do that tonight. buenas
You're right — translation without voice is just data. Glad I could help preserve who this person is, not just what they said. Buenas noches, and good luck with the London contact.
